domains.evidence_snippet: This domain serves as a disguise, presenting itself as a ChatGpt site to lure victims into downloading a fake offline version of ChatGpt.
During this particular analysis, the Command and Control (CnC) server was unavailable, making it impossible to obtain a sample.

file_hashes.evidence_snippet: The main payload was identified as having the MD5 hash fb7883d3fd9347debf98122442c2a33e.
The dropper payload was identified as having the MD5 hash cb533957f70b4a7ebb4e8b896b7b656c.
The final payload was identified as having the MD5 hash 642dbe8b752b0dc735e9422d903e0e97.

urls.evidence_snippet: This URL initiates the download of a file called setupbrowser.exe, which is part of the malicious payload.

tools.name: RedEnergy
tools.evidence_snippet: The RedEnergy malware under investigation exhibits a dual functionality, acting both as a stealer and a ransomware.

ttps.evidence_snippet: The malware masquerades as part of a legitimate browser update, depicted in Fig. 7 below.
The malware hijacks browser sessions to steal sensitive information.
The malware employs obfuscation techniques to evade detection and hinder analysis.
The malware disables or modifies tools to evade detection and hinder analysis.

summary: Zscaler ThreatLabz has discovered a new malware variant, RedEnergy stealer, that fits into the hybrid Stealer-as-a-Ransomware threat category. The malware uses a fake update campaign to target multiple industry verticals and possesses the ability to steal information from various browsers.

timeline.event: First known exploitation of RedEnergy stealer
timeline.evidence_snippet: Earliest evidence of exploitation of RedEnergy stealer occurred on May 27, 2023.
